worth_it_explanation: Budget pacing checks find money that is in the wrong place. A top campaign that runs out of budget early in the day loses auctions it would have won, while a weaker campaign keeps spending on conversions that cost more than they return. The checks read from the budget status, the budget report, and the impression-share-lost-to-budget column, all of which are already in the account. The fixes are budget moves rather than rebuilds, so they take effect within the same billing period and the result shows in the conversion and cost-per-acquisition (CPA) columns the practitioner already watches.
intro: Budget pacing is about whether each campaign's daily budget matches its value to the account. A campaign that converts well but caps out before the day ends leaves demand on the table. A shared budget pulls from a single pool, so a low-return campaign in the pool can drain spend that a higher-value campaign needed. These checks compare where the budget sits against where the return is, using the 'Limited by budget' status, the budget report, and the share of impressions lost to budget.

audit_checks.title: A top-performing campaign sits in a 'Limited by budget' state
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A campaign flagged as limited by budget is turning off ads before the day ends, so it loses auctions it could win. When the capped campaign is one of the account's strongest by return, the lost auctions are the most valuable ones, and the lost conversions never appear in any report because the ads never served.

educate.base:
Google Ads marks a campaign 'Limited by budget' when its average daily budget is too low to enter every auction it qualifies for. When the budget runs out for the day, the campaign stops serving and resumes the next day. The campaign is not broken. It is choosing not to compete in auctions it could win because the money for the day is already spent.

The cost of that cap depends on which campaign carries it. A weak campaign held back by budget loses little. A strong campaign held back by budget loses the auctions that would have returned the most, and those losses are invisible in the standard reports because the ads never entered the auction. The signal for the size of the loss is the 'Search lost IS (budget)' column, which is the share of impressions the campaign missed specifically because the budget ran out. A high value there on a high-return campaign points to demand the account is paying to ignore.

Raising a budget is not free. More budget on a campaign running a target return-on-ad-spend (ROAS) or target cost-per-acquisition (CPA) strategy gives the bidding more room, but it can also push into less efficient auctions as it scales. The judgment is whether the campaign's current efficiency leaves headroom to spend more at an acceptable return.

A few signals point to a budget cap worth acting on:
  • The campaign shows the 'Limited by budget' status on the campaigns page
  • 'Search lost IS (budget)' is a meaningful share of available impressions
  • The campaign's conversion value or return ranks among the account's strongest
You may want to compare the capped campaign's return against the campaigns it would draw budget from before moving money. A cap on a strong campaign is an opportunity. A cap on a weak one is the budget working as intended.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, the campaigns most worth checking for a budget cap are the ones tied to high-margin products or to the brand terms that convert at a low cost. You may want to confirm whether a capped shopping or search campaign is losing impressions on the products with the best return since the budget ceiling there costs revenue rather than just clicks. Seasonal demand can move a campaign into a cap with no change to the budget itself, so a campaign that paced fine last month can run out early during a sale.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation account, a budget cap on the campaign that produces the cheapest qualified leads is the one to trace first. You may want to confirm the capped campaign's leads convert downstream rather than just filling the form because raising budget on a source of low-quality leads scales the wrong outcome. A campaign capped during business hours, when the sales team can act on leads, costs more than the same cap overnight.

investigate.intro: Find the campaigns marked 'Limited by budget', then check whether any of them rank among the account's strongest by return and how much impression share the cap is costing.

google_ads_ui.label: Google Ads UI
steps: Open the 'Campaigns' page and look at the 'Status' column. Note every campaign that reads 'Limited by budget'.
Add the 'Search lost IS (budget)' column from the column options so you can see the share of impressions each campaign missed because of its budget.

For each limited campaign, compare its conversions, conversion value, and cost per conversion against the rest of the account to judge whether it is a strong campaign being held back or a weak one being capped correctly.
Open 'Budget pacing insights' on a limited campaign to read Google's own status and any pacing recommendation it surfaces.

google_ads_budget_report.label: Google Ads budget report
steps: Open the budget report from the 'Shared library' or the campaign's budget, and read the projected month-end spend against the budget.
Check whether the campaign is projected to spend its full budget every day, which confirms the cap is active rather than occasional.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per campaign, the budget status, the 'Search lost IS (budget)' value, and a rank or note on the campaign's return relative to the account. A campaign with a 'Limited by budget' status, a meaningful share of impressions lost to budget, and a strong return is a fail recorded with the campaign name as a candidate for more budget. A limited campaign with weak return passes since the cap is doing its job.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the budget status, the share of impressions lost to budget, and the relative return for each campaign above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one of {{site_name}}'s strongest campaigns is running in a 'Limited by budget' state, so it stops serving before the day ends and loses auctions it could win. The 'Search lost IS (budget)' column shows the share of impressions the cap is costing, and because those auctions never run, the lost conversions never appear in any report. {{voice_recommend}} raising the budget on this campaign toward the level its impression-share loss implies, watching the cost per conversion as it scales to confirm the added spend holds an acceptable return. {{voice_recommend}} moving that budget from a campaign with weaker return where practical, rather than only adding to the account total.

audit_checks.title: A shared budget lets a low-return campaign starve a higher-value one in the same pool
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A shared budget is one pool that several campaigns draw from. When a campaign with weak return spends first, it can leave a higher-value campaign in the same pool short of budget, so the account funds the worse outcome and starves the better one. The misallocation hides inside a single budget line that looks fully spent and on pace.

educate.base:
A shared budget is a single average daily budget that several campaigns draw from. Google moves money within the pool toward the campaigns that can spend it, which helps when the campaigns share a goal and a return profile. It works against the account when the campaigns in the pool have different value because the budget flows to whichever campaign fills auctions first, not to whichever campaign returns the most.

The result is a pool that reads as healthy at the budget level while misallocating underneath. The shared budget shows as fully spent and on pace, so nothing on the budget line signals a problem. Inside the pool, a campaign with a low return-on-ad-spend (ROAS) can consume the spend a higher-value campaign needed, and the higher-value campaign shows as limited even though the pool as a whole had money.

Google's guidance pairs shared budgets with portfolio bid strategies so the bidding and the budget share the same goal. A pool that mixes a target-CPA campaign with a maximize-clicks campaign, or groups campaigns with very different conversion values, is the setup most likely to misallocate because the campaigns are not competing on the same terms.

A few signals suggest a shared budget is misallocating:
  • The pool groups campaigns with clearly different conversion value or return
  • A high-value campaign in the pool shows as limited while the pool spends in full
  • The pool mixes bid strategies that do not share a goal
You may want to compare each campaign's return inside the pool against the others before deciding whether the grouping still fits. Splitting a high-value campaign onto its own budget is one way to stop the pool from starving it.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, a shared budget that groups a high-margin product line with a clearance or low-margin line lets the line with looser targets absorb spend the high-margin campaigns needed. You may want to confirm the campaigns in each pool share a margin profile, or consider giving the highest-return product campaigns their own budgets so a sale on low-margin stock does not drain them.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation account, a shared budget that mixes a campaign producing qualified leads with one producing cheap but low-intent leads sends spend toward whichever fills auctions first, which is often the lower-intent one. You may want to confirm each pool groups campaigns by lead quality rather than only by theme, so the budget follows the leads that close.

investigate.intro: List the shared budgets, see which campaigns sit in each pool, and check whether campaigns of clearly different value are grouped together while a high-value one runs limited.

google_ads_ui.label: Google Ads UI
steps: Open the 'Shared library', then 'Budgets', to list every shared budget and the campaigns attached to each one.
For each pool, look at the member campaigns' conversions, conversion value, and cost per conversion side by side to see whether they share a return profile.
Check whether any high-value campaign in a pool shows the 'Limited by budget' status while the shared budget itself spends in full, which is the sign of a campaign being starved inside the pool.
Note the bid strategy on each campaign in a pool. A pool that mixes strategies with different goals is the most likely to misallocate.

google_ads_budget_report.label: Google Ads budget report
steps: Open the budget report for a shared budget and read how its spend splits across the member campaigns.
Confirm whether the spend concentrates on a lower-return campaign while a higher-return one in the same pool falls short.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per shared budget, the member campaigns, each campaign's return, and which campaigns show as limited while the pool spends in full. A pool that groups campaigns of clearly different value, where a high-value campaign runs limited while a lower-value one spends freely, is a fail recorded with the pool name and the starved campaign. A pool whose campaigns share a return profile and goal passes.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the member campaigns, their returns, and the limited status for each shared budget above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that a shared budget on {{site_name}} groups campaigns of clearly different value, so a lower-return campaign in the pool is drawing spend that a higher-value campaign needed. The shared budget reads as fully spent and on pace, which hides the misallocation, while the high-value campaign shows as limited even though the pool had money. {{voice_recommend}} regrouping the pools so each one holds campaigns with a similar return and a shared bidding goal, and giving the highest-value campaign its own budget where the grouping cannot be fixed. {{voice_recommend}} pairing each shared budget with a portfolio bid strategy so the budget and the bidding work toward the same target.

audit_checks.title: Budget is concentrated on low-return campaigns while higher-return ones go underfunded
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When the largest budgets sit on campaigns with the weakest return, the account spends most of its money at its worst efficiency. The campaigns that return the most stay small, so the account cannot scale the spend that works. The waste is gradual rather than a hard failure, which is why it can persist across reporting periods.

educate.base:
Budget allocation drifts. A campaign that performed well a year ago can keep its large budget after its return falls, and a newer campaign that converts efficiently can stay on a small budget because no one moved money to it. Over time the account can end up spending the most where the return is lowest, which is the opposite of where the money should go.

The pattern shows when budget share is compared against return. A campaign that holds a large share of the account's spend but sits near the bottom on return-on-ad-spend (ROAS) or near the top on cost per acquisition is absorbing money that would do more elsewhere. The campaigns at the other end, strong return on a small budget, are the ones that could scale if the spend moved to them. The 'Search lost IS (budget)' column on the underfunded campaigns confirms there is demand left to capture.

This is a reallocation question, not a pause-everything question. A weak campaign can still serve a purpose, for example holding presence on brand terms or feeding an upper-funnel goal, so the judgment is whether its spend matches its job. The aim is to match each campaign's budget to the return it produces, then move the freed budget to the campaigns that can use it at a better rate.

A few signals point to budget concentrated in the wrong place:
  • A campaign holds a large share of account spend and a weak return
  • A campaign with strong return runs on a small budget and shows impressions lost to budget
  • Budget shares have not moved even though the return ranking has
You may want to rank the campaigns by budget share and by return together, so the campaigns that hold spend they do not earn stand out against the ones that earn spend they do not have.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, budget can stay concentrated on a product line that sold well in a past season while a line with a better current margin runs underfunded. You may want to rank the shopping and search campaigns by both spend share and return on ad spend, so the campaigns funding last year's winners stand out against this year's. Moving budget toward the higher-margin lines improves account-level return without increasing the total spend.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation account, budget can sit on a campaign that produces a high volume of cheap leads while a campaign producing fewer but better-qualified leads stays small. You may want to compare campaigns on cost per qualified lead rather than cost per form fill since the concentration looks fine on raw lead count and only shows when lead quality is included.

learn_more.source_title: About overdelivery and your average daily budget

investigate.intro: Rank the campaigns by budget share and by return at the same time, then find the campaigns that hold spend without the return to justify it and the ones earning a return without the budget to scale.

google_ads_ui.label: Google Ads UI
steps: Open the 'Campaigns' page and add the cost, conversions, conversion value, and cost-per-conversion columns, plus 'Search lost IS (budget)'.
Sort by cost to see which campaigns hold the most spend, then read each one's return next to its spend share.
Flag any campaign that holds a large share of spend with a weak return, and any campaign with a strong return running on a small budget while losing impressions to budget.
Open 'Budget pacing insights' to read Google's reallocation recommendations across the campaigns.

google_ads_budget_report.label: Google Ads budget report
steps: Open the budget report to see projected spend by campaign for the month.
Compare the projected spend on the weakest-return campaigns against the headroom on the strongest-return ones to size the reallocation.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per campaign, its share of account spend and its return, then identifies mismatches. A campaign holding a large spend share with a weak return, paired with an underfunded campaign that has a strong return and impressions lost to budget, is a fail recorded as a reallocation candidate. An account whose budget shares track its returns, with the most spend on the strongest campaigns, passes.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the spend share and the return for each campaign above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s budget is concentrated on campaigns with weak return while several higher-return campaigns run underfunded. The account is spending most of its money at its worst efficiency, and the campaigns that convert best stay small, so the spend that works cannot scale. The 'Search lost IS (budget)' column on the underfunded campaigns shows there is demand left to capture. {{voice_recommend}} matching each campaign's budget to the return it produces, moving budget away from the high-spend, low-return campaigns toward the strong campaigns that are losing impressions to budget. {{voice_recommend}} making the change in steps and watching cost per conversion as the stronger campaigns scale, so the reallocation holds its efficiency.
